misconceptionSummary: Students look at the digit being rounded rather than the digit one place to its right.

lines.text: Decision digit: one place further right → 7

steps.narration: Count from the decimal point to find the target place. For two decimal places that is the hundredths digit — four. The decision digit is immediately to its right: seven.

visuals.ruleText: Decision digit 0–4 → round down (leave target digit unchanged). Decision digit 5–9 → round up (add 1 to target digit).
visuals.subText: Then drop all digits to the right of the target place

steps.narration: Seven is five or more, so round up. Add one to the hundredths digit: four becomes five. Drop everything to the right.
Six point eight four seven rounded to two decimal places is six point eight five. The seven triggers the round-up and is then dropped.

visuals.label: Decision digit 7 ≥ 5 → hundredths 4 rounds up to 5

steps.narration: Six point eight five. Two decimal places — the answer stops at the hundredths column.

misconceptionStrip.text: Common mistake: looking at the target digit (4) to decide rounding. The decision always comes from the digit one place to the RIGHT of where you are rounding to.
misconceptionStrip.audioNarration: Never look at the target digit itself. Look one column further right — that digit alone decides whether you round up or keep it the same.
